chunk_summary: Adolescent PCOS diagnosis requires stricter criteria due to pubertal mimics, with emphasis on menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism. Metabolic risk clinics should screen for insulin resistance and comorbidities, linking symptoms to actionable interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand diagnostic criteria for adolescent PCOS, including menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism.
Identify metabolic comorbidities associated with PCOS, such as insulin resistance and dyslipidemia.
Learn appropriate laboratory evaluations and referral criteria for severe or uncertain cases.
Explore lifestyle interventions to mitigate metabolic and androgen-related risks.
key_facts: Adolescent PCOS diagnosis requires persistent menstrual irregularity beyond expected pubertal maturation and clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism.
Pelvic ultrasound is not recommended for diagnosis until 8 years post-menarche.
Screen for cycles <20 days or >45 days persisting at least 2 years after menarche, hirsutism, severe acne, androgenic alopecia, and rapid weight gain.
Initial lab tests include total/free testosterone, TSH, prolactin, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (if nonclassic CAH is suspected).
Evaluate metabolic comorbidities with fasting lipids, fasting glucose, HbA1c, and BP measurement.
Lifestyle changes like refined carbohydrate reduction, improved sleep, and activity can reduce cardiometabolic risk and androgen-related symptoms.
reference_quote: The 2023 international evidence-based guideline recommends that adolescent PCOS diagnosis requires both persistent menstrual irregularity and clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, after excluding mimics.
